# tokens: # def-node-binding — what `def x <node>` binds, and how inner words
# resolve. AQL has DIVERGENT semantics by node kind:
#   - a List def is a Forth-style SPLICE body whose words are LATE /
#     dynamically resolved at every reference (no closure);
#   - a Map def is an EAGER value snapshot whose words are resolved
#     ONCE, at def time.
# Neither forms a closure — only `fn` / `=>` capture lexical scope.
# Runs against the production lang registry (test/go/langspec).
# Expected values use the canon renderer (containers are space-
# 1. What does `x` leave on the stack? (shape of the residual)
# A scalar binds a single value; a List def SPLICES its elements onto
# the stack (it is a body, not a List value); a Map def pushes one
# value. Wrapping a reference in `[ … ]` re-collects a spliced list.
def x 42 x
note: scalar def → one value

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] x
note: LIST def splices its elements (three stack items, not a List)
wrapping the reference re-collects the list

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] x x
note: splices on every reference

# tokens: def x [] [x]
note: empty list splices to nothing
only the outer level splices; inner lists stay values

# tokens: def x {a:1 b:2} x
note: MAP def pushes a single value (no splice)

# tokens: def x {a:1 b:2} size x
note: a map reference is a usable Map value

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] typeof x
note: typeof hits the first spliced element — proof of the splice

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] length x
note: `length` sees a bare Integer, not a List (the reported failure)

# tokens: # 2. Inner words resolve to their def'd VALUES (not kept as words)
def c1 10 def x [c1 2 3] [x]
note: list: inner word resolves to its value

# tokens: def c1 10 def c2 20 def x [c1 c2] [x]
note: all inner words resolve

# tokens: def c1 10 def x {a:c1 b:2} x
note: map: inner word resolves to its value

# tokens: def x [foo 2 3] [x]
note: an unknown inner word is an error, not data

# tokens: # 3. Scope: List = LATE / dynamic, Map = EARLY / snapshot, no closures
# A list stores the WORD and re-resolves it at every reference, against
# whatever binding is current — redefining changes the result, undef'ing
# it errors, and a forward reference resolves at use time.
def c1 10 def x [c1 2] def c1 20 [x]
note: list re-resolves at use → sees the NEW binding (late/dynamic)

# tokens: def c1 10 def l [c1] undef c1 [l]
note: list word resolved at use; once undef'd it errors

# tokens: def x [later 2] def later 7 [x]
note: list resolves a forward reference at use time

# tokens: # A map captures its values once, at def time.
def c1 10 def m {a:c1} def c1 20 m
note: map snapshots the value at def time (early)

# tokens: def c1 10 def m {a:c1} undef c1 m
note: snapshot survives undef of the source word

# tokens: # Lists do NOT close over a fn param / local: the word is re-resolved
# AFTER the scope exits, so it falls back to the module binding (or
# errors). Contrast `fn` / `=>`, which DO capture lexically.
def c1 1 def mk fn [[c1:Integer] [List] [[c1]]] mk 9
note: returned list ignores param c1=9 — falls back to module c1=1 (no capture)

# tokens: def mk fn [[c1:Integer] [Function] [([] => [c1])]] def f (mk 7) f
note: contrast: a `=>` lambda DOES capture the param (c1=7)

# tokens: # 4. Consequence: nested list-defs flatten through splicing
def a [1,2] def b [a 3] b
note: `a` splices inside `b`, which splices on reference → flat

# tokens: def a [1,2] def b [a 3] [b]
note: re-collected: the nested splice flattens into one list
